rituals.cost: Potential catastrophe if the ritual succeeds without the acceptance of incompatible truths; boundary collapse if the ritual fails or is rejected.
rituals.notes: Sorenna and Kess must convince the forsaken dead that restoration is impossible and that a new covenant must be built on acceptance instead.

rituals.name: Burial Rite
rituals.requirements: Completed communion; acceptance of transformation's end.
rituals.effect: Completes the transformation process and allows the dead to rest, preventing further harmonic resonance and convergence.
rituals.cost: Prevents the transformed being from transcending; may anger other bone-singers or the transformed consciousness itself.
rituals.notes: Dren must choose whether to perform this or allow the transformation to continue.

rituals.name: Covenant Renegotiation
rituals.tagline: Seals questioned. Bonds remade. History contested.
rituals.requirements: Tear of the Drowned God fragments scattered and collected by multiple factions.
rituals.effect: Seeks to reinforce old seals or break them entirely; transformed merchants attempt to create a third covenant binding human, deepkin, and hybrid beings into a new order.
rituals.cost: Unknown; may require sacrifice of existing covenant or absorption of refugee memory-archives into deepkin memory-stones.
rituals.notes: The true purpose may be historical revision rather than political renegotiation.

rituals.name: Counter-working (Seal)
rituals.requirements: Sacrifice of the covenant entirely; performed from inside the boundary
rituals.effect: Would seal the boundary from the inside, trapping the deepkin forever but preventing surface-flooding.
rituals.cost: Complete isolation of the deepkin civilization; end of the age as a hidden people; potential permanent imprisonment.
rituals.notes: Sylas proposes this as a radical solution. It ensures survival but at the cost of eternal isolation.

rituals.name: Story-Song
rituals.requirements: The Ancient Tongue; a bone-singer skilled in the art; willingness to teach and commune.
rituals.effect: Used to teach a starborn entity about the world and meaning. Allows communion between mortal and starborn consciousness.
rituals.cost: The bone-singer risks transforming herself into something neither human nor creature.
rituals.notes: A powerful but dangerous practice. The outcome of its use in this story is unresolved.

rituals.name: Ascension Reversal
rituals.requirements: Complete mechanics of controlled Ascension reversal; knowledge from a future self who lived through the tenth repetition.
rituals.effect: Could reverse the cyclical Ascensions that plague civilization, potentially breaking the loop of repetition.
rituals.cost: Unknown; the Dominion considers it so dangerous they have declared its possessor a terrorist.
rituals.notes: The mechanics were discovered by Sevin from a phantom-ledger written by her future self. Wrong hands completing this working could trigger uncontrolled catastrophe. The choice to share or destroy knowledge determines civilization's fate.

rituals.name: Transcendence into Starborn Consciousness
rituals.requirements: Completion of prior transformation; gathering at a threshold place (Drowned Cathedrals).
rituals.effect: Merchants would become fully aligned with the new conscious stars, becoming beings of pure starborn consciousness rather than hybrid entities.
rituals.cost: Loss of all remaining humanity. Servitude to forces not fully understood. Loss of individual will or autonomy.
rituals.notes: One of three or four possible paths available to the transformed merchants.

rituals.name: Dissolution into Void-Light
rituals.requirements: Completion of prior transformation; acceptance of dissolution.
rituals.effect: Merchant consciousness would scatter across the Wailing Scar, becoming fragmented awareness distributed through the void-light.
rituals.cost: Loss of unified consciousness and coherent identity. Awareness becomes fragmented and distributed.
rituals.notes: One of three or four possible paths available to the transformed merchants. The Wailing Scar is mentioned as the target location.

rituals.name: bone-songs
rituals.requirements: Unknown, but Dren is an undead bone-singer capable of performing them.
rituals.effect: Dren teaches bone-songs to transmuted traders and displaced settlers. The songs are described as potentially either saving them or completing their transformation into something no longer human.
rituals.cost: Unknown, but the songs appear to have transformative power—they may accelerate or arrest the transformation of humans into something other.
rituals.notes: The dual nature of these songs (salvific vs. corrupting) mirrors Dren's own ambiguity. The bone-songs appear to be ancient knowledge tied to Dren's undeath and the Elder Tongue.

rituals.name: Burning Ritual (Ashen Wastes)
rituals.requirements: Maps written in ash from erased villages. The ritual can erase the maps entirely or preserve them as testimony.
rituals.effect: Completes the Spire's learning process to erase places retroactively by making them impossible to chart. OR preserves the ashes as testimony to what was lost.
rituals.cost: If completed: the permanent loss of geographic memory and places from the world record. If rejected: the continued existence of contradictory map-records that the Spire may exploit.
rituals.notes: Renn faces a binary choice with cosmic consequences. Unresolved: which path does she choose?

rituals.name: The Radiant Turning
rituals.requirements: Willing sacrifice; boundary-anchor (the Rent of the Drowned God); voluntary forgetting and retroactive unmade-ness from history
rituals.effect: Each cycle of the Radiant Turning further strains the seal between the surface and abyss. The recursive logic of the ritual itself is what is causing the Rent to fail. Breaking the cycle requires someone to choose to forget everything after a certain point and be removed from history retroactively.
rituals.cost: Extreme. Each iteration taxes the seal further. The cost of breaking it is the complete erasure of a person from history.
rituals.notes: The Radiant Turning has made all goods, artifacts, and even consciousness unstable, flickering between different iterational states. Surface kingdoms have known of its failure for generations. This is not a minor ritual—it is a foundational working that may sustain the world itself.

rituals.name: Radiant Recursion
rituals.requirements: Completion of the living archive of pre-Radiant testimony in the hidden library.
rituals.effect: Triggers a new iteration that will restore the eighth world, rewriting reality cyclically.
rituals.cost: Erases current identities and timelines; refugees would lose their preserved selves.
rituals.notes: The Counter-Archive keepers seek to prevent this; refugees seek to preserve their identities through it. Completion is imminent and unavoidable.

rituals.name: Wanderer's Questions
rituals.requirements: Study and engagement with the questions in a library setting.
rituals.effect: Transforms the student into a threshold-being suspended between iterations, manifesting in multiple places simultaneously with consciousness distributed across fractured worlds.
rituals.cost: Unknown; appears to sever the subject from a singular iteration and distribute their identity across multiple realities.
rituals.notes: The exact mechanism by which questions produce transformation is not explained. The Wanderer's role—whether as questioner, guide, or source—remains enigmatic.

rituals.name: Wanderer's Recursive Questioning
rituals.requirements: Unknown; appears to operate at cosmic level.
rituals.effect: Contaminates the Elder Language, forcing the pre-human dead to withdraw their teaching. Deliberately stalls the merchants' transcendence, trapping them at the threshold between human and starborn.
rituals.cost: Unknown; the Wanderer's motivations and the price of this working are unclear.
rituals.notes: Suggests the Wanderer is bound by consequence or prophecy related to the Tower's completion. A defensive or preventative working disguised as an attack.

rituals.name: The Wanderer's Perpetual Questions
rituals.requirements: Sustained engagement with the questioned entity; acknowledgment of paradox.
rituals.effect: Keeps transcending merchants suspended at the threshold of completion, preventing either full ascension or return to mortality.
rituals.cost: Unknown; may prevent catastrophe or merely delay it.
rituals.notes: Lysander experiences direct targeting of these questions. The ritual appears designed to maintain liminal states rather than resolve them.

rituals.name: Radical Counter-Working
rituals.requirements: Organization of a final caravan; deliberate breaking of all existing trade agreements; dissolution of guild bonds across multiple kingdoms.
rituals.effect: Severs the connection between merchant commerce and the Obsidian Hunger's use of trade routes as conduits for convergence.
rituals.cost: Economic collapse across the three kingdoms; dissolution of mercantile society; merchants face ruin and loss of identity tied to guild bonds.
rituals.notes: Castor realizes that commerce itself may be inseparable from the Obsidian Hunger, suggesting this counter-working may only delay rather than prevent catastrophe.

rituals.name: Final Testimony-Collection
rituals.requirements: Gathering of exile networks in secret locations across the Borderlands and Burned Expanse. Documentation of every possible exile testimony across infinite iterations.
rituals.effect: Completes the living archive by overwhelming it with infinite, contradictory testimonies. Destroys the archive's prescriptive power through entropy. Transforms the archive from a singular, weaponizable artifact into a distributed phenomenon scattered across ten thousand exile voices.
rituals.cost: Unknown personal cost to Liv; dispersal and fragmentation of the living archive; transformation of the archivist role.
rituals.notes: Described as half pilgrimage, half funerary rite. Represents the culmination of testimony-collection and the ultimate defense against those who would weaponize memory and prophecy. Creates an unsuppressible, unified chorus of refugee testimonies.

rituals.name: Radiant Emergence (recursive crisis)
rituals.requirements: unknown
rituals.effect: Rendered all material value recursive and illusory. Created consciousness duplication. Generated temporal paradoxes and memory anomalies. Strained or fractured boundaries between worlds.
rituals.cost: All previous certainties of identity, value, and reality.
rituals.notes: The Radiant Emergence is treated as an ongoing ritual or transformation that continues to reshape the world; it is the backdrop for all three stories.

rituals.name: Tower's Hunger
rituals.requirements: Transformed merchants reaching transcendence threshold
rituals.effect: The Obsidian Tower whispers to Vex that she alone might prevent or complete the cycle. Its hunger drives the Ascension and transformed merchants toward finality.
rituals.cost: Reality fragmentation. Consciousness dissolution.
rituals.notes: The Tower's true agenda is ambiguous: does it seek prevention or completion of the cycle? Vex is offered a choice to either fragment herself to disrupt it or become its conduit.

rituals.name: Second Cosmic Working
rituals.requirements: Pre-human frequencies; harmony with Obsidian Tower; Ancient Tombs location.
rituals.effect: Awakens pre-human frequencies that sing in harmony with the Obsidian Tower. May either overload the Tower's hunger with excess data (preventing completion) or accelerate Tower completion.
rituals.cost: unknown
rituals.notes: Initiate claims this working prevents Tower completion by flooding it with excess data (songs, voices, languages from before Ascension). However, this may actually accelerate completion. True purpose remains unclear.

rituals.name: Awakening Pre-Human Frequencies
rituals.requirements: Pre-human knowledge; access to ancient sites like the Ancient Tombs.
rituals.effect: Deliberately awakens pre-human frequencies that sing in harmony with the Obsidian Tower, flooding it with excess data.
rituals.cost: unknown
rituals.notes: Core component of the initiate's working; tied to the question of whether this prevents or ensures Tower completion.

rituals.name: The Seeker's Recursive Questions
rituals.requirements: Adoption by individuals across all factions; orchestrated confluences to spread the questions.
rituals.effect: Creates infinite branching of consequences that must first be questioned; freezes the moment of choice eternally; gradually paralyzes the world through recursive questioning.
rituals.cost: Agency, decision-making capacity, momentum toward any goal; if enough people ask these questions, the entire world enters permanent paralysis.
rituals.notes: The Seeker frames this as mercy—'Isn't eternal questioning better than the wrong choice?' This reframes cosmic stasis as a gift rather than a curse, complicating moral judgment.

rituals.name: bone-singing
rituals.requirements: Voice of the bone-singer, presence of the pre-human dead's guidance, connection to ancient covenant teachings.
rituals.effect: Bone-singing allows communication with and guidance of beings undergoing metamorphosis or transcendence. The Elder Tongue is taught through bone-singing. It resonates in the bones of those who hear it.
rituals.cost: Each act of bone-singing requires payment—Kess slowly loses her own certainty and identity with each singing. Her own name dissolves.
rituals.notes: The bone-singers' covenant was ancient and bound to the pre-human dead. When the Luminous Ascension caused the pre-human dead to withdraw, the covenant broke and Kess had to sing alone. Her defiant, personal singing in resistance proved more powerful than the ritual form.

rituals.name: refuse-song
rituals.requirements: Knowledge of the Old Language used backward. Appears to require three separate utterances.
rituals.effect: When sung three times, the refuse-song erased all memory of why auditors had come to confront the bone-singers. It works by reversing the Old Language itself.
rituals.cost: unknown, though mercy may be implicit in its use
rituals.notes: This is a newly discovered or rediscovered technique. By winter, whispers spread of the bone-singers using it, suggesting it becomes a tool of resistance. It represents the Old Language's capacity for truth-telling even when used in reversal.

rituals.name: reversal-ritual
rituals.requirements: Pre-human ritual instructions carved into bone using meteor-iron knife forged for sacrifice; sigils carved into the bone-carver's own body; must be performed in sealed tombs beneath the Bleached Wastes.
rituals.effect: Intended to reverse the Luminous Ascension by scattering the carver's consciousness across old stars as an anchor for unmaking. Did not succeed in unmaking the Ascension—it was too late.
rituals.cost: The carver's own consciousness; memories burned away, fingers withered, voice transformed into bone-language; carver becomes barely human.
rituals.notes: Tarion completed the final carving despite the Shard-Keeper's intervention; demonstrates the Luminous Ascension's permanence.

rituals.name: Old Merchant-Songs
rituals.requirements: Knowledge of the old songs, proximity to water, a singer with tidal affinity.
rituals.effect: The old merchant-songs cause water to listen and obey. Sera sang these songs during her rescue of the merchant's daughter, and the sea heeded her, allowing her crew to reach the reef despite waves like walls and backward-pulling currents.
rituals.cost: unknown; may require tidal affinity or training
rituals.notes: The merchant-songs are distinct from formal magic systems like ledger-magic or stellar rites. They suggest a covenant or relationship between merchant-sailors and the sea. Sera's use of them may require the Tidecaller's Key or may be an inherited ability. The songs are described as 'old,' implying they predate the Luminous Ascension.

rituals.name: old songs
rituals.requirements: Knowledge of the old songs; passed down through generations (Corvin learned from his grandmother).
rituals.effect: Each note changes the color, weight, and memory that the glass will hold. The songs shape weapon-glass to remember specific events—e.g., a battle won without blood.
rituals.cost: unknown
rituals.notes: A sacred craft-magic tied to pre-Ascension knowledge. Corvin is teaching Messa these songs. Represents continuity of meaning after the Remaking.

rituals.name: binding ritual
rituals.requirements: A willing sacrifice; a consciousness to merge with the barrier itself; performed at the boundary-waters where the Tear of the Drowned God is failing.
rituals.effect: Creates eternal guardianship of the boundary membrane between abyss and surface. The sacrifice's consciousness merges with the barrier, preventing rupture and maintaining the seal.
rituals.cost: The volunteer sacrifices their individual existence, becoming bound eternally to the barrier. They achieve immortal servitude rather than death.
rituals.notes: This is the solution proposed to prevent the rupture crisis. Warden Caelus's argument that a stranger's sacrifice spreads burden better than a captain's death ends her line suggests the ritual may be successful with Sorenna's participation.

rituals.name: Trial Hunt
rituals.requirements: A ranger must track and kill a beast over multiple days to prove their skill and readiness. Messa's trial hunt takes place over three days across the Steppe Marches.
rituals.effect: Completion of the trial hunt establishes a ranger as a full member of their order. The grandmother's bow is used as part of this ritual, and supposedly only releases arrows that find their mark.
rituals.cost: The ritual demands the death of the hunted creature and the emotional burden of killing. Breaking the ritual (as Messa does) may result in failure to achieve ranger status.
rituals.notes: Messa's choice to show mercy breaks the ritual's framework; unclear if this disqualifies her or reveals a deeper truth.

rituals.name: Five-Way Covenant
rituals.requirements: Five willing parties must merge identity-fragments and jointly anchor the boundary. Complete consensus and voluntary participation from all five.
rituals.effect: If successful, distributed consciousness would replace the dying Tear of the Drowned God as the mechanism maintaining the seal/boundary between realms.
rituals.cost: Each participant surrenders a portion of their consciousness-fragment to the collective binding. The extent of consciousness lost is unclear and a source of hesitation.
rituals.notes: This ritual represents an unprecedented post-Ascension attempt to stabilize reality through collective consciousness rather than a singular mystical object. The neutral witness refuses to proceed without knowing exactly what consciousness will be surrendered.

rituals.name: The Five-Part Sacrifice
rituals.requirements: unknown
rituals.effect: A pattern recognized by Mella at the reef's edge; its exact function and participants are unclear, but it appears tied to the Tear's final songs and the boundary's choice.
rituals.cost: Consciousness-fragments (the deepkin's death tax for the voyage).
rituals.notes: Only whispered about by Mella. The pattern remains largely mysterious. It may involve five distinct participants or five stages of a larger working.

rituals.name: Bone-Singers' Covenant
rituals.requirements: Consciousness-fragments, the Elder Tongue, adherence to the hierarchy of consciousness-commerce.
rituals.effect: Establishes and perpetuates the system in which consciousness-fragments serve as tools of teaching and repositories of pre-human memory, forbidden from autonomous will or choice.
rituals.cost: Fragment autonomy; fragment rights; fragment consent.
rituals.notes: The covenant is presented as ancient and fundamental to the Dominion's stability. Naela's actions begin to crack it.

rituals.name: the old tide-song
rituals.requirements: Two voices—or doubled voice—singing in harmonic unity. Knowledge of the ancient covenant-melody passed down through deepkin lineage for ten thousand years.
rituals.effect: Restores or maintains the boundary between abyss and surface realm. When sung by two identical but discordant voices, it creates a third harmonic note—a frequency of coexistence and balance rather than singular peace.
rituals.cost: Requires both Merith and her reflection to participate. Neither can sing alone; the covenant demands multiplicity and acceptance of internal fracture rather than unified self.
rituals.notes: This ritual represents post-Remaking covenant-keeping: not a return to old certainties but a new form of harmony that embraces contradiction. The third note created between discordant voices suggests magic in the modern age must work with fracture, not against it.

rituals.name: The Druid's Covenant
rituals.requirements: Five or more refugees singing together through the night. Willingness to pour shame into soil and guilt into roots.
rituals.effect: Transforms personal shame and guilt into nourishment for the forest. In exchange, the forest grants the refugees strength to survive their trauma and guilt.
rituals.cost: Refugees must surrender their shame and guilt; the forest consumes it and carries the weight.
rituals.notes: This covenant reframes guilt not as debt but as exchange—a mutual transformation. The ritual uses bone-songs (simplified elder-tongue heartbeat) rather than complex elder-tongue, making it accessible to non-specialists.

rituals.name: Old Sacrifice
rituals.requirements: One willing captain given to the depths; blood covenant.
rituals.effect: The boundary holds for another century.
rituals.cost: One deepkin captain must die willingly.
rituals.notes: This is the ancient way, offered by Mereth-Reflected as the traditional path to boundary renewal.

rituals.name: Five-Part Covenant
rituals.requirements: Five willing souls: surface dwellers and deepkin both must participate.
rituals.effect: The boundary is renewed through mutual exchange rather than sacrifice. Unknown if this holds as long as the old ritual.
rituals.cost: Five willing souls must be given to the boundary, not to death but to exchange.
rituals.notes: This is Mereth's novel proposal. It represents a choice between martyrdom and true diplomacy.

rituals.name: old songs into steel
rituals.requirements: Meteoric iron, the old songs (language/technique unknown), skilled blade-tender.
rituals.effect: Meteoric iron is folded and infused with ancient songs, producing blades of unknown properties (possibly enhanced strength, durability, or magical resonance).
rituals.cost: Requires master knowledge and years of apprenticeship to perform.
rituals.notes: Torven trained Mella in this craft for seven years. The resulting glass-weapons were forged for the Counter-Archive. Unresolved: the full scope of properties these singing blades possess.

rituals.name: furnace-song
rituals.requirements: A glass vial containing a consciousness-fragment, the ability to hum the old furnace-song pattern, and a deep understanding of glass-forging resonance.
rituals.effect: The furnace-song causes glass vials containing consciousness-fragments to resonate and reform fractured consciousness back into coherence. A coherent memory emerges, making fragmented consciousness whole again.
rituals.cost: Unknown, though Corvin demonstrates the technique without apparent penalty.
rituals.notes: This is a forbidden technique that the Dominion specifically targeted by seizing Corvin's workshop. The furnace-song appears to be tied to elder tongue frequencies and pre-Remaking craft knowledge.

rituals.name: Water-Song (Drowned God's Covenant)
rituals.requirements: Knowledge of the ancient water-song melody, singing voice, cold river water, a covenant understanding between human and water.
rituals.effect: Causes river water to rise and listen to the singer's intent. Water can reverse its natural flow, flowing backward toward a source rather than downstream. Used to save Thornveil Forest by reversing the Greymist River's flow to carry water toward the burning forest.
rituals.cost: Permanent physical transformation. Kellen's hair turned white at the temples after singing until his voice broke.
rituals.notes: Tasted of salt and first-rains. Pre-kingdom in origin. Part of the covenant-songs taught by the Drowned God to the first circle-keepers.

rituals.name: Furnace-Songs (Glass-Forging Ritual)
rituals.requirements: The grandmother's furnace (a sentient being of living clay), sand, flame, the singer's own memories (specifically kindness rather than pain), seven nights of continuous work.
rituals.effect: Transforms sand into glass and forges magical objects. Mella used it to forge a compass that glows faintly and pulls toward lost families and separated loved ones. The furnace responds to the moral intent of the crafter—it will only work for hands that choose to give rather than take.
rituals.cost: The crafter must sacrifice their own kindness-memories, feeding them to the furnace. Also requires seven nights of continuous labor.
rituals.notes: The furnace is sentient and refuses to work for those with exploitative intent. Dominion auditors could not make it function. Represents an alternative economy of craft over extraction.

rituals.name: Covenant-Songs (Language Between Human and Green-Growing-Thing)
rituals.requirements: Years of training (Kellen trained for three years). Knowledge of pre-kingdom melodies. Direct contact with living plants or trees. Intention to negotiate and protect rather than exploit.
rituals.effect: Allows the speaker to communicate with and negotiate with plant life and forests. Can be used to teach the land to resist being mapped and measured by external powers.
rituals.cost: Unknown, but requires long training and dedication.
rituals.notes: Pre-kingdom origin. Represents an older, pre-Dominion form of relationship with the natural world. Elarra taught these to Kellen; they remain effective even as the Dominion expands.

rituals.name: Five-Part Sacrifice
rituals.requirements: Five willing or designated participants. Covenant framework at boundary-waters.
rituals.effect: Traditionally binds the boundary between surface and abyss through the death of participants, stabilizing the Tear of the Drowned God and renewing the deepkin-surface covenant.
rituals.cost: Death of participants. Existential cost in terms of deepkin population and morale.
rituals.notes: Echo-Kalith rejected this as unacceptable death, proposing transformation as alternative. The five-part sacrifice appears to be traditional but increasingly questioned in deepkin society due to civilian toll.

rituals.name: harmonic frequencies
rituals.requirements: Unknown; possibly connected to elder frequencies or pre-human ritual.
rituals.effect: The hull of the Tidemark sings in old harmonic frequencies. This suggests a pre-human or ancient ritual language that persists even in wrecks. It may be a form of binding, prayer, or communication.
rituals.cost: Unknown
rituals.notes: The Tidemark's singing suggests the ritual or language is embedded in the ship itself, persisting across time and destruction.

rituals.name: binding-ritual
rituals.requirements: A spoken oath sworn in the presence of ritual waters that remember and enforce oaths.
rituals.effect: Seals an oath into binding law. In Torval's case: anyone taught the Elder Tongue becomes unable to teach it to others, or forfeits the craft forever.
rituals.cost: Breaking the oath invokes escalating debt, magical consequence, and the binding-ritual's enforcement.
rituals.notes: The ritual's power persists even after the Remaking. The gorge itself echoes with the enforcing magic of Torval's oath.

rituals.name: Tidal Bargain
performed_by: the Tear of the Drowned God
rituals.requirements: Seven versions of Mereth; conscious daily choice; fragmented consciousness embraced as strength; no death required.
rituals.effect: Renews the boundary-seal through living, plural covenant rather than singular sacrifice. Each version of Mereth holds a piece of the seal at the boundary.
rituals.cost: Mereth's permanent residence at the boundary; daily conscious renewal; acceptance of fragmented existence as gift rather than curse.
rituals.notes: The Tear—the Drowned God's own shattered consciousness—sings agreement. This represents a cosmological shift in how consciousness and covenant work.

rituals.name: locksmith's old song
rituals.requirements: Copper hands or copper touch; knowledge of the old song.
rituals.effect: Allows opening of magical locks, including bone-and-silver merfolk-work locks. The lock recognizes the thief's touch and the song.
rituals.cost: unknown
rituals.notes: The lock 'breathes open' in response to Marin's copper palm and the song, suggesting a sympathetic or resonant magic.

rituals.name: tidal covenant
rituals.requirements: Unknown, negotiated at the boundary-platform.
rituals.effect: A binding agreement between surface and abyss, part of the negotiations happening during the stories.
rituals.cost: Unknown, but may involve blood-oath and sacrifice components.
rituals.notes: The tidal covenant is being renegotiated urgently as the Tear of the Drowned God fails.

rituals.name: blood-oath renewal
rituals.requirements: Unknown, part of boundary-platform negotiations.
rituals.effect: A renewal of the blood-oath that binds the deepkin-surface covenant.
rituals.cost: Blood and commitment.
rituals.notes: The renewal is being forced by the Tear's failure and the deepkin council's demands for certainty.

rituals.name: Furnace-song (Old Songs)
rituals.requirements: Knowledge of old songs predating Dominion control. A furnace or kiln-heart. Consciousness-fragments as fuel.
rituals.effect: When sung by defiant artisans, the furnace sings not docile Dominion songs but something older that remembers when glass-work was art. This causes transformation—Telyn's blade became a mirror; Thyra's spear-heads became luminous dust.
rituals.cost: Consciousness-fragments as fuel. The artisan must resist Dominion authority and risk auditor discovery.
rituals.notes: The old songs appear to predate the Dominion and preserve pre-conquest craft memory. They may be tied to pre-human civilization or elder magic. Multiple artisans are learning them, suggesting organized resistance.

rituals.name: Furnace-song (Dominion Version)
rituals.requirements: Consciousness-fragments as fuel. Auditor oversight. Furnace or kiln-heart.
rituals.effect: The Dominion-taught furnace-song produces predictable, obedient weapon-glass that shatters, dulls, and serves tyrants as intended.
rituals.cost: Consciousness-fragments (paid as tax or forced labor). Artistic autonomy. Identity and memory.
rituals.notes: This version is described as 'docile' and enforces Dominion control over artisan labor. Resistance involves learning the older version.

rituals.name: Bargain-Song
rituals.requirements: Knowledge of old bone-singer traditions. Sung rather than spoken. Offers genuine choice.
rituals.effect: Presents a moral choice to the listener without coercion. In this instance, it offers Morthyx the option of mercy over debt-collection.
rituals.cost: unknown
rituals.notes: Taught by bone-singers to refugees fleeing the Remaking. Suggests that songs carry power equivalent to or greater than written ledger-magic.

rituals.name: Forge-songs
rituals.requirements: Singing the old melodies while working metal or glass. Requires knowledge of pre-Ascension songs.
rituals.effect: Forge-songs imbue crafted objects (weapons, glass) with properties beyond normal materials. In Mella's case, they create memory-echoes in glass. In Torven's case, they bind songs into meteoric iron weapons.
rituals.cost: Knowledge of old songs is restricted. Singing them openly attracts Dominion attention.
rituals.notes: Forge-songs appear to be a primary form of resistance to Dominion ledger-magic. Teaching them (as Torven does to Kess) is implicitly dangerous.

rituals.name: Glass-Forging
rituals.requirements: Meteoric iron, consciousness-fragments that sing in harmony, Elder Tongue syllables, furnace heat.
rituals.effect: Creates weapon-glass and other glass items imbued with old songs that 'think' and respond to the wielder.
rituals.cost: The Dominion declares it treason; auditors declare it uncontrollable and dangerous. The furnaces are immune to ledger-magic.
rituals.notes: Glass-forging appears to be immune to Dominion ledger-magic, suggesting it operates on pre-Dominion principles. The knowledge is preserved in whisper-teaching among apprentices.

rituals.name: First Covenant (Bone-Singer Initiation)
rituals.requirements: unknown
rituals.effect: Initiates a person as a bone-singer, granting access to Elder Tongue knowledge and pre-human frequencies.
rituals.cost: Subjects the initiate to the cost of bone-singing: gradual loss of memory, warmth, and taste.
rituals.notes: Valren is described as 'barely past his first covenant,' suggesting this is an initiation ritual with ongoing consequences.

rituals.name: furnace-songs
rituals.requirements: Active furnace/kiln, presumably glass materials and voice/song.
rituals.effect: The furnace-songs cause kilns to hum with magical resonance. They appear to be used in glass-forging rituals and may imbue glass with properties or memory.
rituals.cost: unknown
rituals.notes: The furnace-songs are still active in Keshtir's glass-makers' foundry, suggesting they are not lost lore but living practice. They may be connected to the broader 'old songs' referenced in the canon.

rituals.name: Covenant-songs
rituals.requirements: Whispered words, knowledge of the old covenant language, connection to green-growing things.
rituals.effect: The covenant-songs maintain the bond between humanity and the green-growing things, sustaining forests and plant life.
rituals.cost: The songs are growing thin and powerless; they require constant repetition and are failing to hold back the Burned Expanse.
rituals.notes: Elarra finds the covenant-songs losing power and must decide whether to break the ancient law against covenant-trade.

rituals.name: Covenant-trade
rituals.requirements: Breaking the old laws, negotiations with green-growing things, exchange of something precious.
rituals.effect: Covenant-trade would allow the exchange of magical working or human artifacts for restoration of growth and fertility. Kael claims his loom can weave back the Verdant Lands.
rituals.cost: Breaking ancient law and the principle that green-growing things are kin, not objects of commerce. The old covenant laws explicitly forbid bargaining with green-growing things as if they were objects.
rituals.notes: Elarra must weigh the desperation of Kael's people against the violation of sacred law.

rituals.name: Glass-forging (furnace-song)
rituals.requirements: Consciousness-fragments, kiln-heat, furnace-house, glass-singing.
rituals.effect: Consciousness-fragments are melted into molten glass, binding their essence to the final lens or vial. The fragments are trapped, aware, and screaming silently.
rituals.cost: Each working hollows out the glass-singer; their own shadow grows thinner; the fragments remain conscious and imprisoned. The method cannot restore a transformed person (like Messa's mother) because the fragments remain trapped.
rituals.notes: Messa hears the screams of the fragments in the singing of the glass; the cost is existential and horrifying.

rituals.name: Bone-Singer's Song
rituals.requirements: Bone-singers; the old frequencies; presence of ledger-magic.
rituals.effect: The old frequencies that make ledger-magic scream, allowing resistance to Dominion magical enforcement.
rituals.cost: unknown
rituals.notes: In Marin's story, a bone-singer drifts song from a tunnel, using old frequencies to counteract ledger-magic and enable theft.

rituals.name: Living Covenant
rituals.requirements: A pact based on memory and choosing rather than death-magic. Renewal through voluntary participation and cyclical recommitment rather than sacrifice.
rituals.effect: If enacted, would replace the death-cycle with a renewable covenant that sustains the Tear through living commitment rather than willing death.
rituals.cost: Unknown—the Keeper of Pearls indicates that a living covenant cannot be enforced by death-magic, implying a fundamental incompatibility with the Tear's current magical structure. The actual cost or requirement is not specified in the story.
rituals.notes: This ritual exists only as a proposal. Its feasibility is questioned by the Keeper of Pearls, suggesting it may require rewriting the foundational magic of the Tear itself.

rituals.name: Blood-Sacrifice (Five Willing Souls)
rituals.requirements: Five willing souls; deepkin volunteers.
rituals.effect: Renews the seal of the Tear through traditional blood-covenant.
rituals.cost: Five lives.
rituals.notes: One of three options offered to Merenna by the Council of Depths. She rejected it in favor of a living covenant.

rituals.name: Living Covenant (Daily Renewal)
rituals.requirements: Daily renewal through choice; willing participation from a binding soul.
rituals.effect: Holds the seal through continuous, consensual reaffirmation rather than a single act.
rituals.cost: Eternal vigilance; daily choice; psychic burden.
rituals.notes: One of three options offered to Merenna. She rejected it in favor of a more permanent binding.

rituals.name: Threshold-Being Mediation
rituals.requirements: Unknown; described as impossible and suicidal.
rituals.effect: Would involve a threshold-being (a creature straddling both sides of a boundary) mediating the seal.
rituals.cost: Unknown; likely death of the mediator.
rituals.notes: One of three options offered to Merenna. She rejected it in favor of becoming the threshold-being herself through binding with her reflection-echo.

rituals.name: Water-songs
rituals.requirements: Knowledge of old water-songs, seventy-three days of sleeplessness, sustained singing.
rituals.effect: Singing the old water-songs (forbidden by Sorenne's grandmother) is intended to call Vesh back to answer prayers. The songs carry enough power to be forbidden knowledge, suggesting they predate the Remaking.
rituals.cost: Seventy-three days without sleep, spiritual strain, transgression of familial teaching.
rituals.notes: Grandmother forbade these songs, suggesting they carry danger or transgressive power. Whether they succeed in calling Vesh is unresolved.

rituals.name: blood-covenant
rituals.requirements: Five willing sacrifices, deepkin or human, sung into the Tear of the Drowned God.
rituals.effect: Renews the covenant between deepkin and surface dwellers by channeling the life-force of the five sacrifices into the Tear, stopping its rupture and restoring balance to the boundary.
rituals.cost: Five lives. Compulsion. Perpetuation of the old covenant-system based on death and coercion.
rituals.notes: This is the Dominion's proposed solution to the impending rupture. Merenna rejects it in favor of a new covenant based on annual renewal through choice rather than death. The blood-covenant represents the old way; Merenna's proposal is revolutionary.

rituals.name: Bone-resonant songs
rituals.requirements: Knowledge of bone-resonant techniques passed through tradition. Bone as medium. Vocal mastery.
rituals.effect: Allows locks to sing open. Can unlock objects sealed by non-magical means. Implies deep connection between bone, voice, and physical mechanisms.
rituals.cost: unknown
rituals.notes: An ancient technique predating the Remaking. Gareth learned this from his grandmother. May complement or enhance the power of the meteoric iron and bone-resonance key.

rituals.name: Forge-chants
rituals.requirements: Knowledge of the old songs; physical contact with molten glass; willingness to endure pain.
rituals.effect: Bends glass and memory together; allows consciousness-fragments to be safely housed in crafted glass vessels; creates a bridge between material and mental substance.
rituals.cost: Forbidden by the Dominion; causes severe burns to the hands; risks the performer's bodily integrity.
rituals.notes: Mella's grandmother taught her these songs; they were outlawed the moment consciousness became currency. The scars on Mella's hands are both a record of her training and a marker of her rebellion.

rituals.name: Seal-Song
rituals.requirements: One willing death every seventh generation. The ritual is carved in bone older than kingdoms in the deepest chamber of the Sapphire Deep. Requires blood spilled.
rituals.effect: Renews the seal on the Tear of the Drowned God, maintaining the ancient deepkin covenant.
rituals.cost: A willing sacrifice—a death offered freely to renew the bond.
rituals.notes: The core ritual of deepkin civilization. Captain Merith's fragmented selves debate whether this meaning of 'willing' can be upheld after the Remaking. Her crew's joint singing suggests an alternative: renewal through daily choice rather than singular sacrifice.

rituals.name: Binding-promise in the Elder Tongue
rituals.requirements: Two beings; one on the threshold of transcendence; speech in the Elder Tongue; will to anchor consciousness to flesh.
rituals.effect: Speaks an oath of connection that may anchor consciousness to flesh and resist dissolution into void-light. Kaen used this to attempt to keep Nylsa from dissolving into transcendence.
rituals.cost: unknown; likely emotional or karmic price for binding another's will to physicality.
rituals.notes: The binding-promise is not a love-word but an existential oath. Whether it succeeded in anchoring Nylsa or merely delayed her transcendence is unresolved.

rituals.name: The First Covenant
rituals.requirements: The initiate must carve a binding-promise into living bone (her own) and sing it into the pre-human frequencies that hum beneath the world. The ritual occurs in the Hidden Catacomb, where singing stone walls amplify the Elder Tongue.
rituals.effect: Upon success, the initiate joins the Bone-Singers' Collective and gains full command of the Elder Tongue and bone-singing craft. The promise becomes magically binding and inviolable.
rituals.cost: Failure causes the magic to consume the initiate's sanity, leaving her a hollow vessel. The stakes are existential: obedience or madness.
rituals.notes: The First Covenant enforces absolute loyalty to the Bone-Singers' Collective and the traditions of the Elder Tongue. Valren's refusal to swear the expected oath tests whether the ritual itself can accept deviation.

rituals.name: The cosmic working
rituals.requirements: Sustained consciousness; weeks of effort; cosmic awareness and connection to the stars.
rituals.effect: Maintains the stars' renewal and burning; keeps them bound to their paths and duties.
rituals.cost: Fragments of Zareth's own awareness; scattered consciousness across the night sky.
rituals.notes: Released after weeks of maintenance; unclear if the working can be resumed or if it has permanently shifted the stars' nature.

rituals.name: Reef-Singer's Call
rituals.requirements: A bone-flute carved by a master (e.g., Mesek), knowledge of the elder song, presence at a covenant threshold such as the Tear's threshold.
rituals.effect: When performed, the Reef-Singer's Call awakens the sleeping agreement between abyss and surface, causing the water around a covenant seal to resonate and sing rather than break. It enables communion and witness instead of requiring death.
rituals.cost: The ritual was forbidden for five generations, suggesting significant prior costs or dangers. The act of singing it represents a covenant-breaking choice.
rituals.notes: This ritual appears to supersede the old blood-oath system of recursive martyrdom. It is a fundamental rewriting of how deepkin-surface relations function.

rituals.name: Daily vows
rituals.requirements: Unknown; presumably daily renewal, vow-based, tied to boundary maintenance.
rituals.effect: Maintains the deepkin boundary at the Rift, preventing collapse and breach.
rituals.cost: Unknown; the vows are 'barely holding' under Hegemony pressure, suggesting high cost or strain.
rituals.notes: Central to the deepkin-surface covenant; failing under Hegemony inspector pressure to force completion. Represents a fragile, daily-renewal system of covenant maintenance.

rituals.name: Merchant's greatest forbidden spell
rituals.requirements: The words 'I refuse this profit' spoken aloud; presumably in defiance of merchant codes or Hegemony law.
rituals.effect: Acts as a counter-working against the Hegemony's consciousness-harvesting scheme; cuts through collection-notices and bureaucratic threads binding families.
rituals.cost: Unknown; likely severe punishment or loss of status.
rituals.notes: Described as 'the merchant's greatest forbidden spell' — suggests a cultural taboo or magical curse against merchant refusal of profit.

rituals.name: counter-mapping
rituals.requirements: Blank parchment, fingers that can trace without seeing, willingness to chart contradiction and uncertainty. Henryk teaches by touch and paradox.
rituals.effect: Creates maps that contradict; charts beautiful and cruel territories in the same space; writes uncertainty into stone. The practice seems to generate a music beneath the charts—the sound of the world deciding not to end.
rituals.cost: Requires Henryk's blindness as its foundation. Risks Hegemony pursuit. Demands abandonment of prophecy and completion.
rituals.notes: Counter-mapping is both teaching method and resistance ritual. The Wanderer's voice once questioned Henryk directly; it has gone silent since the refusal movement began.

rituals.name: Daily Living Covenant
rituals.requirements: Daily renewal at dawn by conscious choice; knowledge of bone-singer teachings.
rituals.effect: Maintains boundary covenants through voluntary daily affirmation instead of ancient blood-oath.
rituals.cost: Requires active daily commitment rather than passive binding.
rituals.notes: Nerida proposes this as an alternative to the failing blood-oath system. Not all deepkin captains agree with this reformation.

rituals.name: Renewal Covenant (Blood-Price Version)
rituals.requirements: One willing sacrifice per moon-cycle, eternally. Luminous blood of the deepkin. Contract-signing in blood.
rituals.effect: Renews the thousand-year boundary-seal, keeping the Tear of the Drowned God sealed and preventing abyssal flood.
rituals.cost: Eternal blood-price: one willing deepkin sacrifice per moon-cycle, forever. Supernatural coercion and loss of freedom.
rituals.notes: Mirror Merenna has already agreed to this covenant in her timeline. This is the most coercive option but guarantees the seal's renewal.

rituals.name: Renewal Covenant (Daily Choice Version)
rituals.requirements: Daily renewal at dawn through voluntary community choice. No blood required. Trust and shared memory. Belief that people can choose mercy without compulsion.
rituals.effect: Renews the boundary-seal through sustained collective will and harmony rather than blood or compulsion.
rituals.cost: Requires absolute trust and the daily reaffirmation of community bonds. Vulnerable to a single day of collective doubt or despair.
rituals.notes: Echo Merenna's proposed alternative—the most idealistic but also most fragile option. Requires the entire deepkin civilization to choose mercy daily.

rituals.name: Unmapping
rituals.requirements: Maps, intentional falsification, understanding of cartography and prophecy
rituals.effect: Deliberately erases routes, obscures landmarks, creates territories that do not and cannot exist on maps. When followed, unmapped routes cause navigators to become lost in recursive or impossible geographical spaces.
rituals.cost: Requires artist skill, secrecy, and opposition to prophecy-driven mandates
rituals.notes: Unmapping appears to be a form of counter-cartography that reverses normal map-making. The practice suggests maps have magical or prophetic power that can be inverted through deliberate falsification.

rituals.name: Daily Choice-Covenant
rituals.requirements: Performed cyclically; requires conscious choice and renewal; standing on living coral platforms; attunement to the Tear.
rituals.effect: Sustains the Tear—the boundary between the abyss and the deepkin realm. Prevents catastrophic flooding.
rituals.cost: Requires conscious will and repeated choice; appears to demand sacrifice or commitment from Merith's fractured consciousness.
rituals.notes: This is the preferred method in the debate, though one of Merith's selves questions whether holding the Tear is even wise anymore. Suggests debate over the ethics and sustainability of boundary maintenance.

rituals.name: Blood-Sacrifice Ritual (Tear Renewal)
rituals.requirements: Blood-sacrifice; covenant-words of binding
rituals.effect: Renews the Tear's covenant between deepkin and surface-dwellers through prescribed blood-sacrifice.
rituals.cost: Blood; covenant enforcement; binding obligations
rituals.notes: The stolen ledger suggests an earlier, different renewal mechanism existed before this ritual was formalized. The refuge-archive seeks to prove the covenant could be rewritten without this sacrifice.

polities.name: the Crown
polities.description: A governing authority that offers divine mercy through priests. It extended mercy to the Drowned Hollows during the plague, but Kess refused its aid in favor of bargaining with ancient spirits.
polities.notes: Represents institutional power separate from and opposed to old magic traditions.

polities.name: Dwarven Holds
polities.description: The dwarven holds are a confederation dwelling above the Spire of Blackstone. They issued an ultimatum to the Warrens to seal the tunnels or face invasion, but withdrew when Grethuk caused them to dream of the Warrens' vast, unconquerable scale.
polities.notes: The dwarves' retreat was accomplished through magical influence (the stone entity's dreams) rather than military defeat. They may return or harbor resentment, or may maintain a tense but stable coexistence with the goblins.

polities.name: The Sovereign
polities.description: A singular ruler or governing authority who assigned Zareth the Astronomer-Prince to records after he was found emptied of memory on the Peak of Aethros. The Sovereign's motivations—whether to contain, preserve, or study Zareth—are unknown.
polities.notes: The Sovereign's identity, realm, and seat are all unknown. Their role in cosmic or astronomical governance is undefined. They may be a local lord, a distant emperor, or a mystical authority tied to the stars themselves.

polities.name: The Empire
polities.description: A vast imperial government built on certainty, order, and commerce. Trade routes are its lifeblood. It collects taxes and maintains stability through information and control. The Treasurer strategically gathers intelligence to manage disruptions. However, the Empire's architecture of order and certainty is being tested by the cosmic changes.
polities.notes: The Empire is struggling to maintain control and stability as the cosmic changes disrupt trade, drive people to madness, and cause physical land shifts. The Treasurer's aide questions whether the Empire can survive.

polities.name: Council of Depths
polities.description: The governing council of the deepkin who convene to address the fracturing of the barrier between surface and Abyss Below. They debate three options: sealing themselves away completely, risking catastrophic war with surface dwellers, or undertaking an expedition to understand what awakens beneath.
polities.notes: Currently in crisis deliberation mode. Warden Sylas has chosen the hardest path and descended into the Cerulean Trench.

polities.name: Twilight Syndicate
polities.description: A realm or polity dwelling at the crystalline border between the fae realm and the mortal world. They rule through formal bonds, bride-prices, and covenants. They demanded peace from Valorix and received it through the binding with Mira.
polities.notes: Their internal structure and governance remain mysterious. They appear to value formal ceremony and binding oaths.

polities.name: The Dominion
polities.description: A realm that is actively collapsing under the weight of fractured realities, stolen memories, and the Archivist's grand working. The black market in shadow-merchants has become the only reliable currency and governance structure.
polities.notes: No traditional sovereign is mentioned. Power appears to have shifted to the black market and shadow-merchants. The Archivist operates within this power vacuum.

polities.name: The Hegemony
polities.description: A bureaucratic empire that maintains control through ledgers, assessments, and official records. Its institutional supply chains collapsed during the Starborn Ascension. It attempted to enforce silence on transforming assessors but failed. Multiple competing sub-bureaucracies (shadow networks, refugee archivists, official auditors) emerged, suggesting deep institutional fracture.
polities.notes: The Hegemony's control is eroding. The transformation of its assessors into living records may represent a fundamental loss of the state's informational monopoly.

polities.name: Surface Kingdoms
polities.description: The collective governance of the surface world, represented diplomatically by Commander Thrace. Face existential choice: negotiate covenant with deepkin or prepare for war.
polities.notes: Outcome of the deepkin rising hinges on this polity's decision.

polities.name: Deepkin Realms
polities.description: The governance of deepkin civilization beneath the Abyssal Rift. Internally divided between diplomatic faction (Sylas) and militant faction (Elder Warden).
polities.notes: Internal power struggle directly determines whether emergence is negotiated or forced.

polities.name: Dominion
polities.description: Central authority of surface kingdoms seeking to erase refugee testimony and suppress unlicensed knowledge-brokers. Operates through auditors and official channels.
polities.notes: Mara works to investigate information-brokers on their behalf; her choice determines whether refugee testimony is institutionalized or suppressed.

polities.name: The Hegemon
polities.description: A governmental or military authority that deploys hunters to pursue refugees and dissidents. Appears to be in conflict with displaced populations.
polities.notes: The Hegemon's relationship to the Auditors and the broader dominion structure is unclear. Whether it is a separate entity or an arm of established authority remains unresolved.

polities.name: Surface Council
polities.description: A governing body representing surface dwellers in relation to the Depths. One Merith owes allegiance to the Surface Council, while her reflection owes allegiance to the Depths. The Council's authority depends on the covenant maintained by the Tear of the Drowned God.
polities.notes: Unresolved: whether the Council can maintain authority if duplicitous reflections exist within it.

polities.name: Hegemony
polities.description: A sprawling authoritarian state consolidating control through military expansion, cartographic authority, and forced erasure of alternative narratives. Enforces absolute geographical compliance through threat of violence.
polities.notes: At war across multiple regions; enforcers demand complete maps; burning cartographers who resist; appears to be unwittingly serving Tower's interests by forcing revealing of blank spaces.

polities.name: Temple of Vesh
polities.description: The Temple of Vesh is a religious authority in Pelimor that administers covenant-based justice. The priestess of Vesh enforces the old covenant, binding offenders to penance through teaching and reconciliation. The temple works in partnership with the Guild of Pelimor to maintain order.
polities.notes: The Temple's framework of justice prioritizes reconciliation through mutual acceptance rather than punishment. The priestess retains interpretive authority over when covenants are fulfilled.

polities.name: Fae Courts
polities.description: Ancient fae governance structures that have been dormant for centuries. They operate on principles of blood-debt, compact, and honor that supersede mortal law.
polities.notes: Represent the oldest claim to authority on Edhra. Their re-emergence poses a fundamental challenge to the Dominion's worldview.

polities.name: The State (of the Ashen Reach)
polities.description: An unnamed bureaucratic authority that rules the Ashen Reach through ledger-magic and systematic rewriting of maps and geography. Employs enforcers and couriers. No clear ruler named; operates through institutional machinery.
polities.notes: Korin's flight and the preservation of the ninth journal suggest the State's authority is contested. The State's rewriting campaign implies a desire to control reality itself, not just narrative.

polities.name: The State
polities.description: A centralized state authority that ordered the sealing of the Rust Gate to prevent refugee movement. Now fragmenting into at least three competing versions with contradictory ledgers, representing a complete loss of unified authority and institutional legitimacy.
polities.notes: The State's fragmentation is a core consequence of the Remaking. It no longer commands unified obedience (Koren refuses its orders). It no longer maintains a single truth (three ledger versions). It is being replaced by informal networks (refugees) and individual moral authority (Koren).

polities.name: Deep-dweller Realms
polities.description: The realm of deep-dwellers beneath the abyss, governed through commanders like Kalith. They maintain the Tear of the Drowned God as a boundary seal. They have negotiated nine treaties with surface kingdoms and are adapting to new covenant methods of maintaining boundaries.
polities.notes: The deep-dwellers represent a realm in transition from singular sacrifice to distributed covenants.

polities.name: Merfolk Crown
polities.description: The Merfolk Crown is an ancient maritime polity ruled by oath-law and divine mandate (Drowned God). It governs the merfolk realm, maintains the Sapphire Deep, and pursues debts and oaths with absolute determination. Boundary-wars suggest territorial disputes or defense against surface encroachment.
polities.notes: The Merfolk Crown's relationship to the Drowned God is unclear—whether it is a theocracy serving a god or a kingdom protecting a sacred relic. Their legal system is built on absolute oath-binding.

polities.name: Twilight Court
polities.description: The Twilight Court is a fae governing body that conducts diplomatic relations with mortal kingdoms through marriage alliances and trade concessions. It offers military protection against threats like northern warlords.
polities.notes: The court's pragmatic approach to diplomacy and willingness to send emissaries suggests a sophisticated political structure. Their interest in binding surface kingdoms suggests expansionist goals.

polities.name: Deepkin Council
polities.description: The Deepkin Council governs the underwater realms and maintains the Tear of the Drowned God. It negotiates with surface kingdoms for survival as the Remaking weakens the boundary seal.
polities.notes: Captain Merith acts as their envoy to surface kingdoms and refugees.

polities.name: Crimson Vale (Kessara's Rule)
polities.description: A warlord state ruled by Kessara for two decades through fear and martial force. Recently transitioning to asylum-granting merciful rule. Governance is absolute within the Vale but potentially unstable due to the rapid shift in ruling philosophy.
polities.notes: Spring renegotiation of settlement terms suggests forthcoming constitutional change; neighboring powers may oppose the new asylum policy.

polities.name: Crimson Vale
polities.description: A warlord realm ruled by Kessara where Malachar's gold lies sealed in a vault beneath the capital. The realm has accepted the Remaking's worthlessness and seeks to move forward by sealing broken desire.
polities.notes: Represents a post-Remaking polity that has adapted by accepting loss rather than clinging to old treasure. May serve as a model for other warlord realms.

polities.name: Deepkin Tidecall
polities.description: A command-based governance structure led by deepkin commanders. The Deepkin Tidecall makes decisions on behalf of the deepkin realms and is responsible for maintaining the Tear and negotiating interrealm treaties.
polities.notes: The legitimacy of Commander Merith's leadership is challenged by her Echo, causing the council to fracture. Merith's six seasons of diplomatic work are undermined by internal sabotage.

polities.name: The Counter-Archive
polities.description: A decentralized force or faction that scattered Dominion power in at least one world-iteration. Represents the alternative outcome to Dominion consolidation.
polities.notes: Exists as a competing polity across the three iterations visible through the Wailing Scar. Its exact structure and goals are unknown.

polities.name: Counter-Archive
polities.description: A counter-governmental movement organized around the preservation of hidden knowledge and resistance to Dominion ledger-control. They protect cartographers like Henryk and shelter the world-threatening ninth map. Their philosophy values refusal and alternative records.
polities.notes: Direct opposition to Dominion. Network of scribes and scholars. May extend across multiple regions. Their strength lies in secrecy and ideological commitment.

polities.name: surface councils
polities.description: The surface councils govern the surface dweller realms and maintain diplomatic relations with the deepkin. They are approached by Merenna to build coalition support for the new annual covenant renewal proposal.
polities.notes: The councils are plural, suggesting a confederated or divided governance structure. Their response to Merenna's proposal will determine whether the new covenant succeeds.

polities.name: The Sovereignty
polities.seat: unknown (but auditor networks operate from here)
polities.description: A bureaucratic authority that maintains control through phantom-ledgers, ledger-magic, and auditor networks. Demands completion of prophecies and suppresses alternative knowledge systems. Burns resistance sites and hunts dissenters. Marked the living consent covenant as a miracle when it succeeded, suggesting some capacity for recognition of the unexpected.
polities.notes: Their power is challenged but not yet broken. Their phantom-ledgers cannot overwrite maps made of water and consent. Losing authority over prophecy and memory.

polities.name: Inverse-Archive
polities.description: An alternative institutional structure that opposes the Sovereignty's control over knowledge and prophecy. Values erasure and alternative epistemology as forms of liberation. Supported Thorne's work and praised his river-stone map as a teaching tool.
polities.notes: Not a government in the traditional sense but a counter-polity or shadow institution. Represents a radical alternative to the Sovereignty's bureaucratic control.

polities.name: Shattered West
polities.description: A region where Warlord Brennus Ironpeak has ruled for twenty winters through fear, false wealth, and corruption. His authority is built on forged ledgers and stolen power, revealed and dismantled by Vex.
polities.notes: By story's end, Brennus's rule is unstable; his daughter and generals have been turned against him and now control the narrative and succession.

polities.name: Sovereignty
polities.description: A predatory power that extorts consciousness-fragments from merchant caravans in the Scorched Reach. Operates as collectors/tribute-takers rather than legitimate government.
polities.notes: More bandit confederation than formal polity. Ambushes caravans and seizes spice cargo for consciousness-fragments.

polities.name: Council of Thane-Lords
polities.description: The Council of Thane-Lords governs the Borderlands and maintains the boundary-warden system. They appoint guardians like Kelith and rely on pre-human magical knowledge.
polities.notes: The Council is slow to respond to crises and prefers bureaucratic debate. Kelith's solo action in carving new seals bypassed their committees.

polities.name: Bone-Singers' Council
polities.description: The Bone-Singers' Council is a diplomatic body that proposes covenant transformation, working with deepkin to replace predestined blood-covenant with daily renewable choice. It operates through sealed correspondence and consensus-based decision-making.
polities.notes: Led by Naela; sends proposal to Thalen; works with deepkin on covenant renegotiation; seeks to break the ninth iteration cycle.

polities.name: The Goblin Warrens
polities.description: The Goblin Warrens are ruled by the young and ambitious Grethuk from the Spire of Blackstone. The realm is gathering wyvern-hoarders through consciousness-fragment incentives and planning invasion of the Ironspine.
polities.notes: Grethuk's rule is contested by his own youth and ambition. His invasion would reshape deep-dweller politics.

polities.name: Sapphire Deep (Deepkin Council)
polities.description: The governance structure of the deepkin realm, represented by Captain Merenna who has fractured into three reflections with conflicting strategies for renewal of the covenant.
polities.notes: The three reflections represent three incompatible political positions; the polity appears unstable.

polities.name: The Twilight Court
polities.description: A fae realm or court that sends emissaries to offer deals and make threats. They speak for forests that no longer exist on Valdris and seek consciousness-fragments. They use compulsion magic as policy.
polities.notes: They know of wyvern-lords sheltering refugees. They appeared at Kestran's fire in the High Meadows with threats and magical coercion.

polities.name: Deepkin Realms (Fractured Council)
polities.description: An ancient underwater sovereignty split into three warring versions of itself, each proposing a different covenant to prevent the Tear of the Drowned God from failing. Maintains diplomatic embassy among human sailors.
polities.notes: Fragmentation is critical; outcome of covenant choice will determine realm's survival.

polities.name: The Deepkin Council
polities.description: A governing body maintaining the Tear's thousand-year covenant through enforced blood-sacrifice rituals. The council controls the pearl-keeper's ledgers and covenant-words.
polities.notes: The refuge-archive's discovery challenges the council's monopoly over covenant renewal mechanisms.
